s_2.text:
The dealer is a heavyset Vellian with four arms, each one busy with a different task — polishing a barrel, counting credits, scratching his neck, and beckoning you closer. His name, according to the sign, is Gruub.

'You look like someone who needs firepower in a hurry,' Gruub says, grinning with too many teeth. 'Plasma pistol. Compact model. Three shots before the cell's dead, but those three shots will put a hole through hull plating.' He names a price — steep, but not unreasonable for hardware that could save your life.

You count your credits. You can afford it, barely.

choices.text: Talk to Zara the information broker
Browse the other market stalls
Head for the north maintenance shafts
Head for the south cargo elevator

s_colour_3.text:
The PA system crackles overhead — a burst of static, then an automated voice cycling through three languages. Docking request. Imperial light frigate, registry number starting with INQ. Not Malachar's corvette. A scout ship.

His advance team is already here.

The market noise dips for a half-second as people register what they heard, then resumes louder than before. A vendor starts packing his stall. Two men near the entrance exchange a look and walk quickly toward the docking ring. Somewhere in the crowd, someone is swearing in Vellian.

The clock just got shorter. Whatever you are going to do, you need to start doing it now.

choices.text: Visit the weapons dealer
Talk to Zara the information broker
Browse the other market stalls before leaving
Head for the north maintenance shafts
Head for the south cargo elevator

s_8.text:
The security office door is sealed, but the lock panel still has power. You examine it — a standard Station Authority biometric lock, but the biometric reader is smashed. Only the keypad entry still works. Someone has scratched four numbers into the wall beside it: 7-7-3-1. A previous scavenger's gift, perhaps.

You punch in the code. The door slides open with a reluctant hiss.

Inside, the office is small and ransacked — chairs overturned, monitors smashed. But in a drawer beneath the main console, you find what you need: a Station Authority security keycard, still sealed in its plastic sleeve. It should open the bulkhead doors to the lower decks.

choices.text: Search the office more thoroughly
Check the back room
Ask to speak with Harko directly
Take the keycard and head for the maintenance shafts

s_9.text:
You dig through the remaining drawers and cabinets. Most contain useless paperwork — duty rosters, incident reports from a station that no longer exists in any meaningful sense. But taped to the underside of the desk, you find a single EMP grenade. Military hardware — someone stashed it here as insurance.

The grenade is compact and heavy in your hand. One use only, but it would fry every electronic system within ten metres. Drones, locks, comm relays. Useful.

choices.text: Check the back room
Ask to speak with Harko directly
Head for the maintenance shafts

s_harko_accept.text:
You nod. Harko does not smile — he is not the type — but something in his expression shifts. Satisfaction, maybe. Or just the look of a man who has found a useful tool.

'Good,' he says. 'When you have something concrete on Malachar — his objective, his timetable, his force strength — you contact me on channel four-seven. My people will open the tunnel.'

He stands, and the meeting is over. One of his heavies escorts you back through the corridor. The deal sits in your gut like a second meal. Harko is practical and reasonable, which makes him more dangerous than a thug. But a private extraction route is worth the price of an obligation.

You are back in Harko's territory. Time to move.

choices.text: Take a look at Harko's operation before leaving
Head for the maintenance shafts

s_market_1.text:
You drift deeper into the market, past the main stalls. The crowd thins slightly here — fewer shoppers, more people with business that does not involve browsing. A narrow lane branches off between two welded containers.

On the left, a stall is hung with objects that do not belong on this station. Carved stones. A ring of dark metal. Something that looks like a compass but is not. A hand-lettered sign reads: PRECURSOR ARTEFACTS — AUTHENTICATED. The dealer is a thin human woman with prosthetic eyes that track you with unnerving precision.

Across the lane, a canteen has been set up in what was once a storage alcove — a counter, six stools, a pot of something that smells almost like coffee. Three scavengers sit at the counter, talking in low voices.

Farther down the lane, you can see a viewport set into the station's hull — one of the few on the middle ring that has not been plated over.

choices.text: Examine the artefact dealer's wares
Sit at the canteen and listen
Look out the viewport
Head back to the main market

s_market_artefact.text:
You stop at the stall and pick up the compass-like object. It is warm to the touch, which it should not be.

The dealer watches you with those prosthetic eyes. 'Precursor navigation aid,' she says. 'Third dynasty. Non-functional — the crystal matrix is cracked. Decorative only.' Her voice is flat and rehearsed.

You set it down and let your gaze drift across the other items. 'I'm looking for something specific. A Precursor navigation device. Intact.'

The change in her is immediate. Her hands go still. The prosthetic eyes dilate — an involuntary reflex, or the closest thing to one.

'I don't deal in intact Precursor nav systems,' she says. Too quickly. 'Those are Bureau-controlled items. Class one restricted.'

'I didn't say I was buying. I said I was looking.'

She is quiet for a long moment. When she speaks again, her voice is lower. 'If something like that existed on this station, and if someone were looking for it, I would tell that person two things. First: the Bureau already knows. Second: whatever is in the lower decks has been there a long time, and the things that guard it have been there longer.'

She picks up a cloth and begins polishing a stone. The conversation is over.

choices.text: Try the canteen
Check the viewport
Return to the main market

s_market_canteen.text:
You take a stool at the canteen counter and order whatever passes for coffee. It arrives in a dented tin cup — dark, bitter, and scalding. Good enough.

The three scavengers beside you are mid-conversation. You keep your eyes on your cup and your ears open.

'...went down to sub-four last week,' the one nearest to you is saying. A wiry woman with burn scars on her forearms. 'Made it as far as the atrium before the crawlers came out of the vents. Three of them. Lost my pack and barely made it back to the shaft.'

'Sub-four is gone,' says the man beside her. Big, bearded, missing two fingers on his left hand. 'Anything below three is crawler territory now. They nest in the ductwork — you can hear them clicking if you put your ear to the walls.'

The third scavenger, a young Qeth with a bandaged arm, shakes his head. 'The crawlers are not the problem. There is something else down there. Below sub-five. I heard it through the deck plates. Something large. Moving slowly.' He pauses. 'It was not mechanical.'

The other two go quiet. The woman finishes her coffee and does not order another.

At the far end of the counter, a man in clean clothes nurses a drink that is not coffee. He has been listening too.

You file the information away. Crawlers in the vents, something worse below. Useful to know.

choices.text: Talk to the man at the end of the counter
Ask the scarred woman for advice
Check the artefact stall
Look out the viewport
Return to the main market

s_market_fixer.text:
At the far end of the counter, the man sits alone. He is not a scavenger — his clothes are too clean, his hands too soft. When you sit down next to him, he does not look surprised.

'You have the look,' he says. 'Someone heading down, not up.' He takes a slow sip. 'I sell information. Specifics. Not the kind of thing Zara deals in — she trades in people and connections. I trade in movements. Patrol schedules. Imperial troop positions.'

He sets down his glass. 'Right now I can tell you this for free, because it is good for business when people survive: Malachar's advance team landed twenty minutes ago. Four soldiers, Imperial Enforcers, not regular Army. They are setting up a command post on the docking ring and they will be sweeping downward within the hour.'

He meets your eyes. 'If you are going to the lower decks, go now. Not in ten minutes. Now. Every minute you spend in this market is a minute they spend getting organized.'

The weight of the data chip in your pocket suddenly feels heavier.

choices.text: Ask about patrol schedules in the lower decks
Thank him and head back to the main market
Head straight for the north maintenance shafts
Head for the south cargo elevator

s_market_intel.text:
You lean closer. 'The patrol schedules,' you say. 'The automated ones in the lower decks. What do you know?'

He names a price. It is not cheap, but it is not as bad as Gruub's markup on the plasma pistol.

You pay. He speaks quickly and quietly.

'Station Authority used to run patrols on sub-levels three through six. Rotating shifts, four-man teams, standard six-hour cycles. When the garrison pulled out, the automated systems kept running for about a year. Security drones on fixed routes — predictable, avoidable if you know the timing.'

He draws a rough pattern on the counter with a wet finger. 'The drones cycle through each corridor every nine minutes. Wait at a junction, count to nine, and move when the drone passes. Simple.'

He wipes the pattern away. 'Below sub-six, there are no patrols. The drones do not go down there. Nothing goes down there. Take from that what you will.'

choices.text: Head back to the main market
Head for the north maintenance shafts
Head for the south cargo elevator

s_market_viewport.text:
The viewport is a metre-wide circle of reinforced glass, scratched and yellowed with age. You press your face close to it and look out.

New Babylon Station curves away from you in both directions — a vast torus of grey metal, pocked with running lights and the blue flicker of welding torches where repair crews work on the hull. Beyond the station, the stars are hard and white against absolute black.

And there, closing from the station's port quarter, you see it. A ship. Angular, dark, running without navigation lights. Imperial design — the kind of silhouette that makes civilian pilots change course. It is still distant, but it is not slowing down.

Malachar's corvette. The main force.

You watch it for ten seconds, maybe fifteen. It grows visibly larger in that time. Fast approach. No attempt at subtlety.

Your stomach tightens. The advance team is already here. The corvette is perhaps three hours out, maybe less. When it docks, the station will be locked down. No ships in or out. No exceptions.

You pull back from the viewport. Three hours to get down, find the Compass, and get out. The math is not encouraging.

choices.text: Browse the artefact stall
Try the canteen
Move toward the docking ring entrance
Return to the main market — no more time to waste

s_market_rumour.text:
The wiry woman with the burn scars catches your eye as you stand to leave the canteen. She glances at the other scavengers, then lowers her voice.

'You are going down. I can tell.' She turns her cup in her hands. 'Listen. Sub-level four, the main corridor splits at a junction marked with yellow paint. Left fork goes to the water processing plant — that is where the crawlers nest. You will hear them before you see them. Clicking, like someone tapping metal on metal.'

She rolls up her sleeve. The burn scars extend past her elbow. 'Right fork leads to the freight lifts. Most of them are dead, but one still has power — intermittent, unreliable. If you can get it working, it drops you straight to sub-six without passing through five.'

She pulls her sleeve back down. 'Sub-five is where I lost my crew. I do not recommend the scenic route.'

She picks up her cup and turns back to the counter. You have been given all the charity she has to offer.

choices.text: Check the artefact stall
Look out the viewport
Return to the main market

s_market_docking.text:
You follow the narrow lane past the viewport and find yourself at the edge of the market, where the corridor widens into a transit hub. From here you can see the entrance to docking ring three — a set of heavy blast doors flanked by faded Station Authority markings.

The blast doors are open. Through them, you catch a glimpse of activity — figures in dark uniforms moving with military precision, setting up portable barricades. Imperial Enforcers. They are not looking in your direction yet, but they are thorough, and they will be soon.

A station worker in a stained jumpsuit leans against the wall nearby, watching the Imperials with the flat expression of someone who has seen occupations before.

'They started twenty minutes ago,' she says, not looking at you. 'Checking IDs on the docking ring. They will be in the market within the hour.' She spits on the deck. 'Every time. Same routine. Lock the exits, sweep inward, take what they want.'

She pushes off the wall and walks away. You have seen enough. The noose is tightening, and you are still on the wrong side of it.

choices.text: Head back through the market
Head straight for the north maintenance shafts
Head for the south cargo elevator

s_harko_tour_1.text:
Instead of heading straight for the shafts, you take a longer route through Harko's territory. His heavy does not stop you — the deal has bought you a degree of freedom here.

The corridor opens into a wide maintenance bay that has been converted into a working operation. Cargo containers line the walls, some sealed, some open. Two of Harko's people — a man and a woman in matching grey jumpsuits — sit at a folding table checking manifests against a datapad. They glance up as you pass, assess you, and go back to work.

Beyond them, a container has been converted into a small market — no signs, no advertising, just a curtain across the opening and a steady trickle of people going in and out. Black market. The kind of place where you can buy things that do not officially exist on this station.

A third crew member leans against the wall near a heavy blast door, eating from a ration pack. He has a shotgun slung across his back and the relaxed posture of someone who knows exactly how much violence is appropriate in any given situation.

choices.text: Check out the black market container
Talk to the guard by the blast door
Check the map on the wall further down
Follow Harko's heavy through the corridors
Keep moving to the maintenance shafts

s_harko_tour_2.text:
You push through the curtain. Inside, the container is dim and cramped, lined with shelves of goods that fell off the back of a cargo shuttle. Stim-packs, ration packs, tools, ammunition, a rack of second-hand clothing. A Vellian woman sits on a crate behind a counter, arms folded.

Nothing you need that you do not already have. But the operation itself tells you something about Harko. This is not a smash-and-grab outfit. This is infrastructure. Supply chains. Organisation. The man is building something that will outlast any single deal.

You browse for a minute, letting your eyes adjust. On a back shelf, half-hidden behind a stack of water purification tablets, you notice a small shrine — a candle, a Qeth prayer stone, a faded photograph of someone's family. Even black marketeers need something to believe in.

You step back out into the corridor.

choices.text: Talk to the guard by the blast door
Head for the tunnel entrance
Head for the maintenance shafts

s_harko_tour_3.text:
You approach the guard by the blast door. He watches you come without straightening up — not hostile, just attentive.

'You're the one Harko just talked to,' he says. Not a question. 'Word travels fast down here.'

He jerks his chin toward the blast door behind him. 'That leads to the deep maintenance corridors. Sub-level three and below. We don't go past this point — Harko's orders. Too many things down there that eat shotgun pellets and keep coming.'

He takes another bite of his ration. 'Last crew that went deep was three months ago. Six of them. Two came back. One of those two stopped talking afterwards.' He chews. 'The other one works for Harko now. Night shift. Doesn't sleep much.'

He looks at you with something that might be pity. 'You're going down there alone?'

You do not answer. He nods as if you did.

choices.text: Check out the black market
Find the tunnel entrance
Head for the maintenance shafts

s_harko_tour_4.text:
Harko's heavy leads you down a side corridor that narrows as it goes. The lighting changes from the jury-rigged LED strips of Harko's territory to the original station fluorescents — most of them dead, a few flickering in irregular rhythms that make the shadows jump.

The corridor ends in a maintenance bay. Tools hang on wall racks, most of them coated in dust. A workbench holds the disassembled guts of an atmospheric processor. In the centre of the bay's floor, a hatch is set into the deck plates — a square metre of reinforced steel marked with strips of orange tape.

'That's your way down,' the heavy says. 'Maintenance shaft. Runs behind the outer hull for about fifty metres, then connects to the sub-level three service corridors.' He taps the orange tape with his boot. 'The tape is Harko's. Means the route has been checked in the last month. When you stop seeing orange tape, you're in unmapped territory.'

He pulls the hatch open. Below, a ladder descends into darkness. The air that rises from it is cold and smells of old metal and something faintly organic.

'Good luck,' he says. He does not sound like he means it.

choices.text: Descend into the shaft

s_harko_tour_5.text:
Further along the corridor, the wall panels have been removed, exposing the station's guts — pipes, cables, structural ribs. Someone has written on the bare metal in white paint: HARKO'S LAW — TAKE NOTHING YOU CAN'T CARRY, KILL NOTHING YOU CAN'T EAT.

Beneath the graffiti, a laminated sheet is bolted to the wall. A map of the sub-levels, hand-drawn and annotated. Most of it is labelled CLEARED or AVOID. Sub-level five is marked with a single word in red: NO.

You study the map for a minute. It confirms what you already suspected — the Void Compass coordinates point to sub-level six, in a section marked on no official chart. The map's annotations stop at sub-level four. Below that, Harko's people have not gone.

A crew member passes behind you, glances at the map, and keeps walking. This is old news down here. Everyone knows what is below. Nobody goes.

choices.text: Continue to the tunnel entrance
Head for the maintenance shafts

s_harko_escort.text:
Harko's heavy walks you back through the corridors toward the main thoroughfare. He is not talkative, but he is observant — he checks each junction before waving you through, and his hand stays near the shotgun on his hip.

As you pass through a narrow section between two cargo containers, he stops.

'Listen,' he says. Not to you — to the station. You stop and listen. Through the deck plates, through the walls, you can hear it: a low vibration, almost below the threshold of hearing. Not mechanical. Not the hum of power conduits or the rhythm of atmosphere recyclers. Something else.

It lasts five seconds, then stops.

The heavy looks at you. His expression has not changed, but his hand is now on the shotgun grip. 'That started about a month ago,' he says. 'Comes and goes. Nobody knows what it is. Nobody wants to find out.'

He starts walking again. You follow, and you do not ask questions.

choices.text: Check out the black market on the way past
Head straight for the maintenance shafts

s_sec_terminal.text:
Behind the main office, a narrow door leads to a back room — barely more than a closet. A terminal sits on a shelf bolted to the wall, its screen cracked but still displaying a cursor. Power is coming from somewhere — probably the same tapped line that keeps the lock panel running.

You tap a key. The screen flickers, then stabilises. The system is running on emergency backup — most functions are locked out, but the duty log directory is still accessible.

You scroll through the entries. Most are routine — patrol reports, maintenance tickets, noise complaints from the market. But the last few entries, dated three weeks before the garrison pulled out, are different.

'Anomalous readings from Research Lab 7, sub-level 6. Seismic activity inconsistent with station operations. Maintenance team dispatched — no report filed.'

'Second team dispatched to Lab 7. Team lead reports unusual electromagnetic interference. Equipment failures. Request for additional personnel denied by Station Command.'

The final entry is short: 'Lab 7 sealed by order of Station Commander. All personnel withdrawn from sub-levels 5 and 6. No further investigation authorised.'

Research Lab 7. Sub-level 6. That is where the data chip is pointing you.

choices.text: Check the evidence locker in the corner
Try the comms unit on the shelf
Leave the security office

s_sec_locker.text:
In the corner of the back room, a small evidence locker is bolted to the wall. Standard Station Authority model — reinforced steel, combination lock. The combination died with whoever set it.

The lock is good, but the hinges are not. You draw your vibro-knife and work the blade into the gap between the door and the frame. The molecular-edge oscillator whines as it bites into old metal. After thirty seconds of careful cutting, the hinge pins give way and the door swings open.

Inside: a dead officer's personal effects, sealed in a clear bag. A wedding ring. A data slate with a cracked screen. A photograph of two children standing in front of a viewport, stars behind them. A half-empty pack of cigarettes — real tobacco, worth a fortune on a station like this.

No weapons. No useful intelligence. Just the remains of someone's life, sealed away by procedure and forgotten by everyone.

You close the locker door and leave the contents where they are. Some salvage is not worth taking.

choices.text: Try the comms unit
Leave the security office

s_sec_comms.text:
On a shelf beside the terminal, a portable comms unit sits in its charging cradle. The unit is cracked and the antenna is bent, but a faint power light pulses on the side panel. Someone left it here in a hurry.

You pick it up and examine it. The transmitter is dead — no way to send. But the receiver might still work if you can patch the antenna connection.

choices.text: Attempt a repair with your repair kit
Leave the security office

s_sec_comms_fix.text:
You open your repair kit and get to work. The antenna connection is corroded but not broken — sealant paste and a new contact strip get it functional. You splice in a replacement capacitor from your spare boards and close the casing.

The comms unit hisses to life. Static, mostly. You cycle through frequencies — dead air, dead air, more dead air. Then, on a military band, you catch something. Fragments of a transmission, garbled by the station's hull.

'...Inquisitor confirms objective is sub-level six... research lab designation... full squad deploying to maintenance access points... authorization to use lethal force on any...'

The signal breaks up. You try to get it back, but it is gone — scattered by the station's metal bones.

Sub-level six. They know exactly where they are going. And lethal force authorisation means Malachar is not interested in witnesses.

You set the comms unit down. The information is not surprising, but hearing it in that clipped Imperial diction makes it real in a way that rumour does not.

choices.text: Check the evidence locker
Leave the security office

s_sec_exit.text:
You step out of the security office and pull the door shut behind you. The corridor is empty. From somewhere deeper in Harko's territory, you can hear voices and the clang of metal on metal — crew at work, maintaining the infrastructure that keeps this section livable.

The keycard sits in your pocket, a rectangle of hard plastic that represents access to places most people on this station have forgotten exist. Time to use it.

choices.text: Ask to speak with Harko
Head for the maintenance shafts
